name: Silas Weir
whyHere: You finished something. Completely. It nearly got you exiled, and you are still not sure they were wrong.

inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Belt Knife
inventory.description: A plain working blade — more tool than weapon, but it has been both.

inventory.name: Hemp Rope (30 ft)
inventory.description: Tarred hemp, knotted every arm's length. The difference between a fall and a climb.

inventory.name: Preserved Trail Parcel
inventory.description: Aldric's smokehouse goods — dried river perch and salted pork, cloth-wrapped and knotted. Holds a week on the road. Two parcels.

inventory.name: Assembled Mid-Weight Spear
inventory.description: A seasoned ash shaft six feet long with the mid-weight iron tip road-lashed at the socket throat — wide-cut blade, cross-guard shoulder, oiled leather binding. Four threads seated: ironsense as anchor, Huginn's Ashwarden ending-sense through the fuller, a shadow-harm focus at the quillon, and an order-sense capacitor closed on Pell's reading idiom. The maker's mark is Pell's. Silas's…
inventory.provenance: Rune-bound at the Fell Pell on World-day 18 evening, with Huginn as witness at the stone ring.

inventory.name: Valve Failure Timeline (note)
inventory.description: A small torn page in Mara's hand — four sleeve blank sales, four failure dates, two customer names. World-day minus-eighteen: Fen's intake. World-day minus-nine: east furlong farmer.

inventory.name: Contact Names Scrap (Sorel, Leth)
inventory.description: A chalk-written scrap from Mara — two names from the old pass circuit with Edge District ties. Sorel: spice-broker, wintered in the Edge District two years. Leth: archivist type, likely still there.

inventory.name: Hard Tallow Block
inventory.description: Rendered and pressed flat — good for leather, boots, rope-ends, and a dozen trail needs.

inventory.name: Oilcloth Fold
inventory.description: Heavy enough to shed a night of rain, light enough to pack small.

inventory.name: Pine-Tar Salve Tin
inventory.description: Dara's recipe — for cracked skin, rope-burn, and anything the cold gets into.

inventory.name: River Whetstone (Pell's)
inventory.description: A smooth river-flat whetstone — Pell's own. It belongs to the bench beside the sword now.

inventory.name: Fendt's Testimony (folded page)
inventory.description: A single page in Fendt's hand — dates, seventh-band designations, the nine-day gap, Grael named as the receiving authority, and the preliminary concern notice receipt date. Now sealed inside the document roll alongside Silas's chain-record, bound with brass wire and Calvar's wax. One copy. The other
inventory.provenance: Written by Fendt; carried by Silas from the Edge District; now sealed with a second hand into a complete record.

inventory.name: Brass Wire Coil (fine)
inventory.description: A coil of fine brass wire from Fendt's shop — used for sealing sample vessels; precise enough for binding or rune-work.

inventory.name: Chalk Dust Tin (wax-sealed)
inventory.description: Brilliant white chalk dust in a sealed tin — for marking surfaces in poor light or wet conditions.

inventory.name: Pre-Transition Filter Cloth
inventory.description: A tight-woven square of filtering cloth, pre-Transition manufacture — can strain contaminated water through multiple passes.

inventory.name: Pilfered Camp Tin
inventory.description: A small tin from the opportunists' pack — likely taken from someone's unattended camp. Contents unknown.

inventory.name: Trade-Knives (pair)
inventory.description: Two small belt-trade knives confiscated from the road opportunists — plain, serviceable, nothing special.

inventory.name: Seventh-Band Lens-Blanks (tin)
inventory.description: A small wax-sealed tin holding seven unfinished lens-blanks nested in folded linen, plus a slip of paper with grinding measurements in two hands — a margin note reads 'seventh-band, confirm before cutting.' Found abandoned in a dry hollow beneath a neighboring market stall.

inventory.name: Copper Wire Coil (rune-work)
inventory.description: A short coil of fine copper wire — for rune-thread seating and binding work on the road.

inventory.name: Chalk Blanks (pair)
inventory.description: Two pressed chalk blanks for marking and rune-work. Plain, solid, road-ready.

inventory.name: Binding Paste (twist-wrap)
inventory.description: Dried binding paste in a paper twist — for setting rune-work in place. Veth added black salt to the purchase; it holds in wet weather.

inventory.name: Waymarker (father's)
inventory.description: A small disc of pale grey material — neither quite stone nor quite metal — with a single groove worn into its face in a closed oval. His father's thumb wore that groove. Both layers seated: the first into Stillwater's Trouble, the second into the Raven's Home warden post. The bond between holder and post is complete. It is warm in the hand in a way stone should not be. Today it was warm at…
inventory.provenance: Left by Silas's father for Ama to hold until Silas came asking. Carried from Cairnhold to the March. Both layers now closed.

inventory.name: Father's Sealed Letter
inventory.description: A folded letter in his father's hand, sealed with a maker's mark — genuine, bone-deep in craft. Written when Silas was four. Kept by Ama until the right moment. That moment is now.

inventory.name: Calling-Slip for Warden Coll
inventory.description: A small folded slip in Ossian's hand — four words, written fast. Gets Silas through Warden Coll's door without an argument.

inventory.name: Letter of Introduction — Warden Coll
inventory.description: A short formal letter in Coll's clear hand — three lines naming Silas Weir's Post Master authority and the Hub Council water committee's standing. Coll's personal seal pressed beside the committee's stamp. Valid for any district-governed contact in the Zone fringe.

deeds.description: Raised a cleaned rabbit from the dead in the upper meadow — no ritual, no preparation, hands and will and the valley's old grain answering
Struck a fair provisioning arrangement with Aldric the smokehouse man — hunting for the village in exchange for food and supplies, and offered to keep watch on what's damaging the river catch.
Silas Weir bound death-runes into the assembled spear at an open-sky fire, with Pell Marsh's maker's mark seated into the blade and Huginn witness at the stone ring.
Coordinated a clean boar kill in the north wood — Corvin landed the strike, Silas held the line and read the ending with a bound spear
Brought down the bull boar on the scrub oak ridge with Pell — a clean hunt despite a misfire in the rune-work.
Delivered a dressed bull and sow to Aldric's smokehouse — a significant haul by village standards, carried down from the north wood by three hunters.
Named the repaired death-bound spear Memory in Pell's forge at dawn — a word that held.
Kissed Pell Marsh at the forge bench at dawn — after a full night repairing Memory together and naming it over old wine.
Persuaded Fendt to commit his testimony to paper and walk to Millbrook — moved the falsified ledger thread from evidence-held to evidence-carried.
Persuaded Calvar — pre-Transition filtration engineer and author of the seventh-band specification — to leave the Edge District and travel to Millbrook to attest against Overseer Grael
Neutralized a relay figure in the cut-lane without violence or alarm — The Ended Threat, Wildcraft, and The Shadow Work braided into a clean exit for Calvar and the document.
Showed mercy to road opportunists after disarming them — named the broad man's grief, gave them provisions, and sent them to honest work rather than leaving them in the mud
Called the key players of the water crisis to Pell's forge for an evening council — Dara, Aldric, Fendt, Mara, and the engineer Calvar, all in one room.
Organized the valley's water-crisis response into four parallel working threads — engineering, logistics, supply, and accounts — with named leads for each, in front of every stakeholder
Sparred Pell at the waygate at first light — belt knife against rune-read blade, came out of it with one real lesson and two feet she said were good
Won a Coliseum bout with a single spoken word — never pressed the weakness, let the opponent decide
Named the road plainly to Veth Ondra — the dead warden post, the Disputed Zone, the valley's water — and offered the compact without embellishment
Seated a ward in the Ent grove circle — a collective working with Pell, Veth, Siol, and Huginn — the grove's own shape as the structure, the Weirmark at the join. The Ent received it.
Named and claimed the Raven's Home warden post as an Ashwarden base — Stillwater's Trouble — planting a working post on the March for the first time in eleven years.
Attended the ending of an old badger in the hollow below the Raven's Home post — sat with it in the plain Ashwarden way, no craft past the clean reading, no ceremony past the presence.

chronicle: The Fell Pell gathering sealed and delegated — Calvar to repair lead, Dara to logistics, Mara to supply and comms, Aldric to accounts, Fendt named to the fellowship, document roll complete and bound for Pip Cotter. Silas and Pell step out into the cold night, the road beginning tomorrow, the forge warm behind them.

The Fell Pell gathering: Pell's brigandine finished with full protective rune-work collar to hem, one radiant mark at the chest from the vial's last scraping, and a partial Shielding Word seated in its outer ring with the promise of completion. Pell's short sword rune-bound with four threads including Huginn's ending-sense and her own ironsense. The forge named the Fell Pell; the fellowship formally opened. Calvar took the repair lead, Dara the logistics, Mara supply and comms, Aldric the accounts; Fendt named to the fellowship and vouched for by Aldric. The document roll sealed with every witness line present, Pip Cotter named as carrier. Silas and Pell settled the road: south lane in the morning, home first, then the Disputed Zone. The coals cooled. The night was quiet.
Silas and Pell sleep, share a last morning meal at the forge, establish a relay protocol with Mara Wells, say their farewells, and step south out of Millbrook at a measured morning hour.
Silas, Pell, and Huginn step through the waygate together at dawn — the road south beginning in earnest.

Silas thanks Tane at the close of the morning bouts, rejoins Siol, and hears her plain assessment of the fight. Siol offers a tour of the Hub — plaza first, Ent circle last — and the scene at the Coliseum reaches its natural end.

The party followed Siol north through the Hub's quieter lanes and arrived at the edge of the Ent grove circle at midday — the path giving way to older ground, the circle of trees ahead, Siol stopping at its edge.

The party stepped through the sixth waygate at the Hub — Siol at the arch's edge, the gate-keeper's instruction plain, the March setting confirmed north-by-northwest. The road to Raven's Home has begun.
(a scene whose summary was lost to a formatting error)

Silas Weir arrived at the Stillwater's Trouble hollow on World-day 19 with Pell, Veth Ondra, and Cassiel Ord — planted Memory at the site's center using three braided crafts with Pell's ironsense as anchor, claimed and named the Raven's Home warden post as his own Ashwarden base, seated two Boundary-Stone wards at the post's crown and the hollow's mouth, led Veth down into the hollow so the eleven-year completion could be witnessed by all four principals, walked the post's interior and named every space — Pell's forge corner, Veth's teaching alcove, Cassiel's keeper's threshold, Huginn's rook, Silas's Maker's hollow — added a shadow-and-boundary ward skin to the walls, invited Veth to demonstrate her March-form threshold-hardening which settled into the base layer, and in the quiet after the work finished, Cassiel named himself keeper of the threshold. The post is claimed, named, anchored, and held.

Silas asked Veth to read the soul bond — she confirmed it plainly, named it rune-in-grain. He announced the road to Cairnhold and the introduction to his mother. The party departed Stillwater's Trouble on World-day 19 afternoon and arrived at Cairnhold's outer district on World-day 23.
The party walks from the western crossing lane to Silas's mother's house in Cairnhold's center district — the road ends at an open door and a chimney smoking.

The party sits with Ama at the kitchen table in Cairnhold — the sealed letter open, the Waymarker seated, the father's work named and understood. Silas promises to return with news from the hollow. Ama folds the letter and gives it back to him for the road.

The evening at Ama's table winds down — Ama shows Veth to the east room, Huginn takes the window ledge, and Silas brings Pell to his quarters. The Waymarker and the father's letter rest on the shelf. The household settles.
Silas and Pell settle into sleep in Cairnhold — the child named, the night complete, morning and its reckoning waiting on the other side of the dark.

Breakfast at Ama's table in Cairnhold — the last meal before the road. Ama names Silas's father's hands. Pell and Veth say their farewells in the register they know best. Silas embraces his mother at the door and steps out with the whole party into the early-spring morning, the Crossing ahead.

The party closes Stillwater's Trouble — three flights of Marrow's Wings, the first Ashwarden attendance, two lessons from Veth, and the hollow known and held — and sets out on the March Road toward the Crossing and Edvar Crane.

The party transits the waygate and arrives at the Crossing — a small road-junction settlement with a mill, a well, boiling-rags on the rail, and the gray-brown tinge of the creek running downstream of the Zone.

Silas Weir met Warden Coll in his registry office and built the full accountability chain — document roll, testimony, signed witness statement with Post Master mark, a shadow-architecture of the failing water system raised from the Waymarker, a permanent two-way shadow-slate bound to the Weirmark seated on Coll's desk, a formal Letter of Introduction sealed with Coll and committee authority, a weekly check-in protocol written into the ledger margin, Maren Ossitide's name given as the one legendary figure the Zone draws near, and Huginn's plain three-sound reading of the crisis: the plans hold weight and will move, but the thing upstream is on its own schedule and the clock has not been seen. The scene closes with the flag near dropping and Silas on the stairs.
Silas thanks Ossian and walks south from the annex back to the waygate marketplace — ZR-OA-7, the suspension order's shape, and the letter all in hand.

Silas offers aid to Renna in exchange for the commission; she declines with even professional courtesy and turns to the work — the boar tusks on her bench, the commission complete, the scene at the stall closed.

turns.player: Find Pell. She's either in a boutique or finishing shopping g.
turns.summary: Silas finds Pell at a hardware boutique in the crescent — she's mid-read on a buckle, unimpressed, and turns to meet him.
turns.narration:
You cut back through the marketplace crescent, past Renna's stall with its dark-green awning, and head toward the cluster of small boutiques at the crescent's northern end. The afternoon light is flat and even, early-spring pale, and the foot traffic has thinned since midday.

You find Pell at the third stall in — a narrow shopfront with pressed-metal clasp-work and belt hardware hanging in rows from a wooden frame. She is standing with her back half-turned to the lane, holding a buckle up to the light between two fingers, reading the grain of it through ironsense the way she reads everything: quietly, from the inside out. She hasn't bought yet. The stall-keeper is waiting with the patient stillness of someone who has learned not to interrupt a smith mid-read.

She hears you before you speak — the slight shift of her shoulders that means she knew the step. She sets the buckle back on the frame without ceremony and turns. 'Nothing worth the price,' she says to you, not to the stall-keeper, which is her version of a farewell to the transaction. She falls into step beside you, close enough that her arm finds yours. 'How did the morning finish?'

turns.player: Ask what she made of the crescent — whether anything was worth stopping for
turns.summary: Silas finds Pell at the hardware boutique — nothing worth buying, mid-grade iron throughout. She wants the morning's account.
turns.narration:
Pell sets the buckle back on the frame without a word of apology to the stall-keeper. She steps out into the crescent lane and falls in beside you, tugging her coat straight.

"Nothing," she says. "Mid-grade iron throughout. Someone's pressing these from a bad run and pricing them like the work is in the finish." She glances back once, then lets it go. "The spice row had decent wax cord. I didn't need it."

She is watching your face now the way she watched the buckle — from the inside out. She wants the morning's account. Huginn drops from somewhere above and lands on the frame of the stall you've just passed. The stall-keeper does not look up. The hardware boutique's wooden door, you notice, swung open a breath before Huginn touched down — as though it had been waiting for something it recognized, and made room. Nobody mentions it. The door eases shut again on its own, quiet as a hand laid down.

Pell raises an eyebrow at you. The road to Edvar Crane and the Crossing is still ahead, and she has not asked about Tane Solr yet — but the question is already in how she's standing.

turns.summary: Silas and Pell move from the hardware boutique toward the well to find Veth — she is near the water-seller's cart, listening to him speak.
turns.narration:
"Veth first," you say, and Pell nods once without argument.

You told Veth to ask after Crane and look for workers or resources — that puts her at the water-seller cluster or somewhere near the well, where the talk runs practical and the people who know the settlement's supply lines tend to stand. You head that direction, Huginn lifting off the stall frame and finding the rooftop above you.

The crescent gives way to the broader lane that runs toward the settlement's center. The well is visible ahead — rope-post, boiling-rags still draped on the rail, the gray-brown smell of the creek faint in the air. Two women are filling jugs at a careful distance from each other, neither speaking. A water-seller has his cart at the lane's east edge, a hand-lettered sign nailed to the axle.

Veth is standing near the cart. She has her arms loose at her sides and she is listening to the seller say something at length. Her expression is the one she uses when someone is telling her less than they think they are.

turns.player: Wait for Veth to finish — give her the room to work
turns.summary: Silas and Pell wait at the edge of the lane while Veth finishes working the water-seller for information.
turns.narration:
You slow your pace and stop a few steps back from the water-seller's cart, giving Veth the room she needs. Pell comes to a halt beside you without being asked — she reads the situation the same way you do.

Veth is listening to the seller speak. He is a broad man with a sun-cracked face, standing behind a two-wheeled cart stacked with clay-sealed jugs. He is talking more than he means to, which is usually what happens when someone listens well enough. Veth's arms are loose at her sides. Her expression is level and unhurried, the kind of attention that makes people feel heard and keeps talking past the point they intended to stop.

You catch fragments. The seller says something about the creek running wrong since the cold broke. He says it in the way a man says something he has been saying for two weeks and has not yet gotten a satisfying answer to. Veth nods once, and he says something else — a name, or maybe a direction.

Pell leans slightly toward you without turning her head. "She's good at that," she says quietly. It is not a compliment exactly — more a plain recognition, the way she names the grain in iron.

turns.player: Let Veth report first — hear everything she learned before you decide what to ask next
turns.summary: Veth reports what Osten gave her — the metallic smell, the timing, and Crane's address at the mill.
turns.narration:
Veth wraps up her conversation with the water-seller in a few more exchanges — she thanks him, he nods, and she turns away with the unhurried walk of someone who got what she came for.

She crosses the lane to where you and Pell are standing and stops beside you, facing the well. She does not look at the seller again.

"His name is Osten," she says. "He sells to the settlement and to travelers, boiled or sealed — takes no position on where the gray-brown is coming from, but he knows when it started." She pauses. "Four mornings after the cold broke. Not the creek's ordinary spring silt. He said it smells different. Metallic, he said, and then he said he wasn't sure if that was the right word for it."

Pell makes a small sound that is not quite agreement and not quite doubt.

"He knows Edvar Crane," Veth continues. "Not well. Crane boards at the mill — the long building on the settlement's north edge. He said Crane doesn't come to the well much. Buys sealed jugs." She glances at the cart behind her. "Which means he has something to seal water into, and he is treating his water more carefully than most."

Huginn shifts on the rooftop above, settling its weight from one foot to the other. One sound, low and flat.

Veth looks up briefly, then back at you. "The mill is north," she says. "We can walk it in ten minutes."

turns.player: Go, but let Huginn lead — follow where it goes, not straight to the mill.
turns.summary: Huginn leads the party north along the channel to the mill's intake — Veth reads the treated water without hesitation.
turns.narration:
The crow lifts from the rooftop without being asked.

You watch it angle north along the lane — not the direct route to the mill but something adjacent. It passes the boiling-rags well, passes the two women with their jugs, and drops into the narrower passage between a grain store and a mender's shed where the light goes flat and the smell of the creek is stronger. Huginn does not circle or hesitate. It moves with the particular unhurry of something that knows where it is going and has no reason to rush.

You follow. Pell falls in half a step to your left, and Veth a step behind that. Nobody speaks.

The passage opens onto a short stretch of packed mud where a water-channel runs along the northern edge of the settlement toward the mill. The channel is low for the season. The melt should have it running full, but it is barely knee-deep. The color is wrong. Not the gray-brown of the creek at the well — this is cleaner. Cleaner than it should be, given everything else. Huginn lands on the far bank and stands there, looking at the water with the still, sidelong attention it reserves for things that are in the middle of something.

Veth says quietly, "It is treating the intake." She means the mill. She means Crane has done something upstream of the wheel to pull the water through before it reaches whatever he uses it for. She does not say this as a surprise. She says it the way she names most things — as a fact that was already there, waiting for someone to look at the right angle.

Huginn gives one low sound. Then it is still.

turns.player: Read the channel with Order-Sense — understand what the intake treatment is before you approach, and what it tells you about what Crane is filtering for.
turns.summary: Order-Sense fails in the thin substrate — the intake treatment's purpose stays opaque; Crane must be met face to face.
turns.narration:
You reach for Order-Sense at the water's edge and the lattice gives you almost nothing.

The substrate here is thin — worked ground, settlement-old, the kind of place where the nanite density has been low for years and what remains is scattered. You feel the craft start, feel the familiar opening of design-sight, and then it closes. The channel is just a channel. The intake treatment reads as a blur — something is there, a purpose, a filter arrangement — but the resolution is not fine enough to tell you what it is for. You get the shape of a lid without knowing what it covers.

Huginn is still on the far bank. It gives one more low sound and does not look away from the water.

Veth steps up beside you. "Thin here," she says quietly, without judgment. She is reading the same absence you are. "We will need to ask him directly."

Pell says nothing, but her gaze has moved to the mill ahead — the wheel turning slow in the low water, a single lamp burning in the ground-floor window despite the afternoon light. Someone is working inside, or waiting.

turns.player: Before you knock — let Huginn cross and tell you what it hears in the ending already present here.
turns.summary: Huginn crosses to the mill's foundation and reads what is ending inside — a slow process, long underway, nothing that hurries itself.
turns.narration:
You hold at the bank and wait.

Huginn crosses without lifting. It walks the water's edge to a fallen plank someone laid as a crossing, picks its way over with the deliberate care of something that has no intention of getting wet, and stops on the far side near the mill's foundation stones. It stands there for a long moment — still, head angled, one eye on the mill's lower window where the lamp is burning.

Then it looks back at you.

Not a sound this time. Just the look. But you have been reading Huginn long enough to know the difference between 'nothing here' and 'something here, pay attention.' This is the second one.

After another breath it gives a single short croak — low, unhurried, the sound it makes when something is ending slowly, not fast. Not a crisis. A long process, well underway. Whatever is finishing inside that mill has been finishing for a while, and it is doing so on its own quiet schedule, aware of nothing that would interrupt it.

Pell does not speak. Veth stands beside you and watches the bird, and then watches you.

turns.player: Cross to Huginn and knock on the mill's door — Crane needs to be met face to face.
turns.summary: The party crosses to the mill and knocks — Crane opens the door.
turns.narration:
You step onto the plank and cross. The wood shifts once under your weight but holds, and you come up onto the far bank where Huginn stands near the foundation stones. The bird does not move aside for you. It simply angles its eye back toward the window, which is the whole of its instruction.

Pell crosses behind you. Veth follows last, her boots finding the same steady center of the plank without looking down. The three of you stand at the mill's north face for a moment — the wheel turning slow to your right, the lamp burning in the ground-floor window, the smell of the channel water close.

The door is plain timber, iron-banded, with a bar-slot on the inside but no lock visible from out here. You knock twice — not loud, not tentative. The knock of someone who expects to be heard.

There is a pause. Then the sound of a chair scraping back from a table. Then footsteps — measured, unhurried, not startled. The bar lifts from the inside and the door opens onto a man who looks at you the way a man looks at something he has been waiting to see, and is not certain whether that means good news or bad.

turns.player: Hold back and read him first with Deathsense — understand what is ending inside this man before you say anything.
turns.summary: Silas reads Crane at the threshold — a slow ending in the chest, a long-held accounting behind the eyes. Crane speaks first.
turns.narration:
You hold the threshold and let Deathsense open — quiet, restrained, the way you would check a patient's pulse before reaching for anything sharper.

Crane is ending. Not today, not this season — but the trajectory is there, plain as a road: something in the chest, something slow, the kind of thing that announces itself in small ways before it begins to shout. You file it without showing it on your face. A man who has been calculating an answer for a long time, Veth said. The Deathsense agrees — he is not in a hurry because he has already done the accounting.

His eyes are steady on yours. He is not frightened of you. He is deciding whether you are useful.

"You are not from the committee," he says, and it is not quite a question. His voice is level, mid-range, the voice of someone who prefers facts to conclusions. He is still holding the door. He has not stepped back to invite you in and he has not moved to close it.

turns.player: Name yourself plainly — warden and wright, here about the water, sent by your father's name and by the Waymarker.
turns.summary: Silas names himself plainly — warden, wright, his father's name, the Waymarker shown. Crane opens the door.
turns.narration:
You give him the plain truth.

"Silas Weir," you say. "Warden of Raven's Home. Wright of the March. I am here about the water — and I was sent by Edvar Crane's name, by a man who left a Waymarker in his son's keeping and told him to find you."

You lift the Waymarker from your coat pocket as you say it — the small pale disc, the worn groove, the faint warmth it holds. You do not hand it to him. You let him see it.

Crane is still for a long breath. The decision behind his eyes finishes itself without showing the working. Then he steps back from the door and opens it the rest of the way.

"Come in," he says. He says it to you, and then his gaze moves once to Pell, once to Veth, and settles on Huginn at the foundation stones. A pause. "The bird too, if it will."

Huginn walks in without being asked twice.

turns.player: Read the mill's interior with Order-Sense — understand the space and what it has been used for before Crane speaks.
turns.summary: Silas reads the mill's interior with Order-Sense — a hidden filtration setup, sealed vessels, a diverted floor channel: Crane has been treating and measuring water here for years.
turns.narration:
The substrate is thin here, the same hollow feeling you noticed at the channel, but you have worked in worse. You let Order-Sense open carefully, drawing on what the space will give rather than pushing for more.

The mill's ground floor resolves into its design. A working mill, yes — the wheel's shaft, the stone housing, the channel-feed to the east — but this floor has been remade for a different purpose. The stone benches along the north wall are not for grain. The faint ring-marks left by sealed vessels, a row of them, are too regular for a miller's work. Filtering cloth has been stretched across the south window at some point — the pegs are still seated in the sill, the wood lighter where the cloth blocked the weather. Someone has been treating water here. Not recently, but regularly.

And there is a second thing: a shallow channel has been cut into the floor's eastern edge, running from the main wheel-feed toward a sealed stone box set into the wall. A diversion. Whatever Crane has been measuring or collecting, he has been routing it separately from the mill's main output for long enough that the cut stone has worn smooth at its edges.

Crane watches you take in the room. He does not ask what you see. He is the kind of man who already knows.

turns.player: Ask first about his father's connection to yours — what Crane was told, and what he has been waiting for.
turns.summary: Crane names Silas's father plainly — Aldric Weir came twenty years ago, asked Crane to watch the water, and left a message: give the boy the record, and tell him the fold will not wait past his arrival.
turns.narration:
You look at Crane directly and ask the question that has been sitting in your chest since your mother gave you his name.

"What were you told about my father?" you say. "And what were you waiting for?"

Crane is quiet for a moment. Not reluctant — gathering. He moves to the north-wall bench and sits on its edge, hands folded on his knees, in the way of a man who has rehearsed an answer so many times it has gone past rehearsal into something quieter.

"Your father's name was Aldric Weir," he says. "He came to me twenty years ago — not about the water. About the fold."

He looks at the sealed stone box in the east wall as he says it, then back at you.

"He told me there was a structure in the upper reach — his word was 'fold,' though I would call it an array. He had read its design. He said it was unfinished and that unfinished was not safe. He said he was going to stop his part of the work, because he had a son and the son was more important than the closing." Crane pauses. "He asked me to watch the water. Not fix it — watch it. Count the years. Keep the record clean."

He nods once toward the stone box.

"I have been counting," he says. "The water started changing four mornings after the cold broke this year. Not silt. Not spring runoff. The fold is moving. It has begun finishing what your father stopped."

Pell says nothing behind you. Veth's gaze has not left the sealed box.

Crane looks at the Waymarker in your coat pocket — or at where it was — and his voice when he speaks again is the voice of someone completing a sentence they started two decades ago.

"He said his son would come," he says. "He said: give the boy the record, and tell him the fold will not wait past his arrival."

Huginn shifts on its crossbeam above. Once. The timing is a comment.

quests.title: The Edge District Ledger
quests.premise: A filtration supervisor named Fendt was marked on the Edge District accountability board by his own superior — and no accompanying record was filed. A keeper of thirty years says she has never seen that happen. Fendt is going to be ruined by a piece of paper that does not exist.
quests.stakes: If you walk away: Fendt is stripped and exiled within the season, the water-filtration failure he was blamed for goes uncorrected because the man who understood it is gone, and Overseer Grael's method — a name on a board, no record, no appeal — is confirmed as something that works. He will use it again. He has used it before.

quests.title: The Door That Closed Behind
quests.premise: A wright who finished something — completely, without pause, without asking if he should — discovers that the thin place was never an entrance. It was the consequence: the world sealed what he made into itself, and him with it, and now what he finished is alive somewhere ahead.
quests.stakes: To understand what he completed, and whether completion was the same as creation — or whether he built a door from the inside and never knew it.

stages.objective: the wrong angle of light — the thin place speaks in the grammar of his craft; read what it remembers of the making, and what it has already unmade since
the exilers were not entirely wrong — find who sent the warning before the council moved, and hear what they saw the work do in the last hour before it finished
what completion costs — the finished thing surfaces, wearing the logic of his hand, and asks to be read; what he made, he must now answer for

routes.author: claim it — name what the finished thing is, take the consequence of having made it, and write the last word the work was missing: himself
routes.unwrite: finish it again, differently — use the craft that built it to unmake it properly this time, and accept that the door closing behind him was always the point
routes.read: refuse both poles — read the work as it asked to be read, let it be what it finished into, and walk forward through the consequence as its maker, not its god

quests.title: The Second Thread
quests.premise:
His father left two threads. The first was in a drawer: the Waymarker, and Ama held it until he came asking.

The second could not be left in a drawer, because a drawer holds NOTES and what his father left was WORK — begun, unfinished, and standing somewhere still. He left it with a craftsman named Edvar Crane, at the Crossing, where all roads meet. Fifteen years ago.

The Waymarkers were not surveyors. They were the last people who knew how a waygate is made — and they had never made one. The Numinous built every gate in the world before the Transition, by touching meaning directly, and then dissolved most of the way out of the world that could have taught anyone else. What the Waymarkers held was the leavings: a practice reconstructed backwards from finished gates by people who could not do what the builders did.

Silas's father was trying to finish one. **There is a half-made waygate.** A fold begun and never closed, standing since before the post fell — neither open nor shut, and a fold that is neither is not stable, it is WAITING. It is near the Crossing. Everything crosses at the Crossing.
quests.stakes:
A half-made fold does not stay half-made forever. It resolves, one way or the other, and nobody alive knows which way or when.

And it cannot be finished by a Numinous, because finishing is not what it needs — it was begun by reaching and it has stopped where reaching ends. **It needs a maker's hand to close it and a warden's contact to let it go, and those are the same man exactly once in a generation.** Silas is a wright who tends endings. That is not a coincidence in his lineage. It is what the lineage was FOR.

stages.objective: Fifteen years is a long time. Learn what happened to the man who held the second thread.
stages.condition: The Crossing is the great centre-market where every road ends. Crane was a craftsman. Craftsmen leave trails: guild rolls, debts, a bench someone else inherited, a mark on a piece of work. Follow those, not a name.
stages.change: Codex: Edvar Crane, established as real — his trade, his standing, and whether he is above ground.

stages.objective: Take custody of the second thread and understand what kind of object it is.
stages.condition: It is not a document. It is a WORK — partly made, with the maker's intent still legible in it the way a half-cut joint tells you what it was going to be. Silas can read it as a wright, which is precisely why his father left it to a craftsman to hold rather than a scholar.
stages.change: A real item enters inventory, unfinished, with its own evolution track. It is not a key and does not open anything yet.

stages.objective: Find out what actually happened where your father worked — and why the work stopped.
stages.condition: The post fell. That is the phrase everyone uses and nobody unpacks. Whether it fell to a failure, an attack, an Accord, or the fold itself is the question, and the answer determines whether Silas is finishing his father's work or correcting it.
stages.change: Codex: what the post was, what it was doing, and what stopped it. A named cause where there was a euphemism.

stages.objective: Reach the half-made fold and read it. Do nothing else.
stages.condition: Deathsense will tell him something is ending here, slowly, and has been for fifteen years. Order-sense will tell him the design is sound and incomplete. **The Double Register is the only way to perceive both at once, and he discovered that braid himself, in play, before he knew it was needed.** No craft yet — just standing there and understanding what he is looking at.
stages.change: The fold becomes a known location with a state that advances on the world count whether or not he returns.

stages.objective: Find the practice. Reconstruct, borrow, or invent what the Waymarkers could not finish.
stages.condition: Three doors and all three should be walkable: **the Numinous**, who built the gates and mostly cannot explain it because they did it by reaching; **the Lattice-Cities**, who maintain gates and understand the measurement without ever having made one; and **the work itself**, which is the door his father chose and the reason the second thread exists. Whichever he uses shapes what the finished fold IS.
stages.change: A new practice enters the character — braided, not taught. Something no existing tradition owns.

stages.objective: Resolve the fold. Both are real completions; neither is a failure.
stages.condition:
To FINISH: close the fold as a maker closes a joint, and there is a new way through the world where there was none — the first waygate made since the Transition, made by a man who is not Numinous, by a route the builders did not use. To END it: let it go, cleanly, as an Ashwarden lets things go — and the danger stops, the work stops, and his father's unfinished thing is finished by being concluded.

**He can do both because he is both.** That is the whole arc.
stages.change: The world map changes, or a standing danger to the Crossing is removed. Either way it is permanent and public.

routes.note: The fold is closed as a made thing. A new gate stands. It is his and it is the first in an age.
The fold is released. The danger is gone, the work is concluded, and nothing new stands where it was.
He finishes it and gives it to someone else — the Numinous, the Cities, the Crossing itself. The making was his; the keeping is not.

Party has arrived at the Crossing — Edvar Crane is here somewhere, holding the second thread of the Waymarker's unfinished work.

The suspension order names sub-registrar Holt and a ZR-OA-7 stamp — a deliberate, specific halt by a nameless authority above the district level. The design is clear: one thread cut, all others allowed to conclude.
Edvar Crane located — boards at the Crossing mill, north edge of the settlement.
Crane recognized the Waymarker and opened the door — the meeting is made.
Crane named the fold plainly — Aldric Weir's name, his charge, and the message he held for twenty years. The record is in the stone box. The fold began moving this spring.

quests.title: What the Water Remembers
quests.premise: Mara Wells has traced the Echo River's poisoning to a single point upstream in the Disputed Zone — a structure the old maps don't show, drawing power nothing alive built. It is not malfunctioning. It is following an order, and it is nearly finished.
quests.stakes: If you walk away: the standoff at the filtration arrays goes live — Millbrook's guards and the Radiant leadership are one bad morning from open conflict over water neither side controls. Whatever the structure was ordered to do finishes without a single living person having chosen it, and finishing is not the same as stopping.

stages.objective: Find where the poisoning actually starts.
stages.condition: Trace the contamination upstream past the Disputed Zone fringe (Mara's testimony, Kit Farrow's account of survey-grid lights at night) OR obtain the falsified purity reports from the Radiant filtration arrays.
stages.change: Codex: the source is a structure, not a spill. Something built this.

stages.objective: Learn what the structure is.
stages.condition: Reach the reclamation site itself, or bring what you've found to the Archive Guardian for identification against pre-Transition record.
stages.change: Codex: a pre-Transition nanite reclamation system, still running, still following a standing instruction — 'prepare the watershed.' Linked to What Wakes Beneath.

stages.objective: Learn what 'prepare' means before deciding what to do about it.
stages.condition: Investigate the instruction directly — read the array's own logic, deathsense what kind of thing it is now, or get someone who was there when it stirred to say what they saw.
stages.change: Codex: the shape of the instruction is named, or confirmed unreadable. Harmonic and Radiant leaders learn the source is not each other's doing — the standoff cools, but the deeper question does not.

stages.objective: Decide what the water becomes.
stages.change: The watershed changes. So does what the district trusts. So, one way or another, does the deeper thing the structure answers to.

routes.verist: Bring the true source before the district, publicly, before either side can keep blaming the other. Truth as the thing that ends a standoff.
routes.lattice: Read the array's own instruction set until it's legible enough to touch — not guessed at, known.
routes.ashwarden: Deathsense it directly. It isn't alive the way a person is, but something in it isn't finished being anything else either. Read what an ending means to a thing that doesn't fear its own.
routes.umbral: Go in unseen. Learn what it's actually doing before anyone upstream decides what story to tell about it.
routes.abyssal: Name what the structure wants — to complete its instruction — and treat that want as real enough to bargain with.
routes.stillhold: Broker the standoff first. Get Harmonic and Radiant to stand down long enough to act on the real source together, before either faction owns the answer.

outcomes.name: The Site Sealed
outcomes.summary: The instruction is stopped cold. The water clears. What it was for is buried with it.
narration: The reclamation site goes dark. Within days the Echo River runs clean, and Millbrook's guards stand down from the filtration arrays without a shot fired.

The Archive Guardian does not forgive this easily — a working pre-Transition system, one of perhaps a handful left running in the world, destroyed rather than understood. He is right to be furious and says so.
Whatever the substrate was preparing this watershed for will now happen somewhere else, to someone who didn't get a choice in it either — the Valley's tremor was never the only one.
The district exhales. It does not yet know what it gave up to get that exhale.
Verist disposition toward you: raised — you gave people the plain truth and a clean ending. Lattice: lowered — you didn't need to understand it, only to stop it.

effects.change: sealed — permanently inert, the instruction halted mid-execution

effects.note: a working pre-Transition system destroyed rather than studied; he will not easily trust your judgment on old things again

effects.text: The reclamation site beneath the Disputed Zone has been sealed. What it was preparing the Echo watershed for will never be known here.
Word spreads that the Valley's water crisis had a built source, not a natural one, and that it has been closed for good.
Foreclosure doesn't stop the substrate waking elsewhere — only here. What wakes beneath continues on its own schedule, everywhere the Valley isn't.

outcomes.name: Let It Finish
outcomes.summary: The instruction completes. The water is not restored. It is something else now.
narration: You do nothing to stop it, or you help it along, and the reclamation system runs its last cycle to completion. The poisoning stops — but not because the water went back to what it was.

The Echo River no longer sickens anyone. It also no longer behaves entirely like water. Fish return to it changed in ways no one has words for yet. Nobody drowns in it anymore, and nobody has explained why.

The Archive Guardian is half-vindicated, half-unsettled — this is exactly the kind of thing he wanted preserved and studied, and exactly the kind of thing nobody asked the district's permission to let happen to their river.
You made the opening choice at the smallest scale the largest arc has to offer. What the water became is shaped, now, by what happens to it next — and by who's watching it.
Ashwarden disposition toward you: raised — you let a thing finish being what it was becoming instead of forcing it back into what it had been.

effects.change: completed — instruction fulfilled; the site has gone quiet on its own terms

effects.note: fascinated and uneasy in equal measure; wants access to study what the water has become

effects.text: The Echo River is transformed, not restored. Something in the water is different now, and no one fully understands what.
The Echo watershed has changed in ways that reach past the Valley — the substrate's waking is no longer only a tremor here.
What the water has become will need tending, or watching, or both — and it will not wait forever to be decided about.

effects.note: engaging the reclamation site advances What Wakes Beneath on the shared clock — the tremor became a stirring, and everyone sees it

outcomes.name: The Instruction Rewritten
outcomes.summary: Neither stopped nor completed — amended. Someone finally reads it well enough to change it on purpose.
narration: You don't shut the system down and you don't let it run blind. You get inside the instruction itself and change one line: prepare the watershed — for what it can safely become, not for whatever it was originally for.
The water clears. It also carries something new — small, useful, unmistakably still there if you know to look. The district gets its river back and doesn't know it got anything else.
The Archive Guardian is, briefly, insufferable about being right that this was worth understanding rather than destroying or ignoring.
This was the hard way. Reaching it took the technical or death-sensed truth of stages 2 and 3, not just a decision at the end — and it shows in what's left standing.
Lattice disposition toward you: strongly raised — you didn't defeat the thing or surrender to it. You read it well enough to argue with it and won.

effects.change: redirected — running an amended instruction, monitored

effects.note: delighted, insufferable, genuinely grateful; will share pre-Transition knowledge more readily now
trusts your judgment on old and dangerous things going forward

effects.text: The reclamation site runs a rewritten instruction now — the water is clear, and something small and new persists in it, watched rather than feared.
The Valley quietly solved a problem no one else in the world has managed to solve yet — and someone will eventually come asking how.
A system that can be argued with can be argued with by someone else, too. Not everyone who learns what you did will want the same outcome.

effects.note: engaging the reclamation site advances What Wakes Beneath on the shared clock — the tremor became a stirring, and everyone sees it

progress: Ossian persuaded — Warden Coll named as the Council Member who can act; a calling-slip in hand and the third floor ahead.
Coll has heard the full briefing — he needs the document roll, a signed witness statement naming Grael, and a check-in protocol before he can move the committee.

Coll received the document roll and Fendt's testimony — the shadow-architecture of the infrastructure demonstrated on his desk. He committed to drafting formal aid requests to the Rootkin and Cogitants tonight, conditional on a signed witness statement in Silas's hand with his Post Master mark.

Aid framework drafted with two conditions: Cogitant contact named before Zone entry, Stillwater's Trouble offered to Rootkin as staging. Coll writing both into the requests before tonight's flag drop.
Letter of introduction secured — Silas now carries Coll's name and the committee's standing into the Zone. Check-in protocol terms remain to be named before the scene closes.
Shadow-slate communication channel established with Warden Coll — check-in protocol terms are the final piece before the scene closes.
Contamination start-date fixed: four mornings after the cold broke. Metallic smell, not silt. Edvar Crane identified at the mill — he is already treating his water, which means he knows something.
Crane confirms the water change began four mornings after the cold broke — not spring runoff, not ordinary silt. The fold in the upper reach is moving and has begun to finish what Aldric stopped.
